file.content:
---
name: coordinator
description: Wire two or more workbench projects together using the provides/consumes capability model — async via message bus, sync via HTTP routing, with the registry as the discovery layer
domain: architecture
type: cross-cutting
triggers:
  - "wire projects together"
  - "project composition"
  - "connect two projects"
  - "add RAG to my app"
  - "share embeddings between projects"
  - "add capability"
  - "capability routing"
  - "project provides"
  - "project consumes"
  - "coordinator"
---

# Project Coordinator

## When to use

When two or more workbench projects need to cooperate at runtime — one provides a capability (search, ingest, notify) and another consumes it. Use this skill to declare those relationships, discover providers, and route requests between projects.

The alternative is building it all into one project. That's fine until the capability grows large enough to want independent lifecycle (deploy, scale, test, replace) — at which point splitting into cooperating projects with declared interfaces is the cleaner path.

## Prerequisites

- Both projects registered in the workbench (`make list-projects`)
- `docs/21-elixir-channel-project-type-composition.md` read for conceptual background

content.content:
30	- `docs/21-elixir-channel-project-type-composition.md` read for conceptual background
31	- Workbench running (`make up`)
32	
33	## Step 1 — Declare what each project provides
34	
35	Edit the project's `project.json` (in its `.workbench/` directory, or patch via API) to add a `provides` entry:
36	
37	```json
38	{
39	  "provides": [
40	    {
41	      "capability": "search",
42	      "http_endpoint": "POST /query",
43	      "bus_channel": "search:results",
44	      "description": "Hybrid vector + keyword search over ingested documents"
45	    }
46	  ]
47	}
48	```
49	
50	Standard capability names: `search`, `embed`, `ingest`, `store`, `notify`, `classify`, `summarize`, `generate`, `route`, `evaluate`.
51	
52	Apply via API:
53	```bash
54	curl -X PATCH http://localhost:3200/projects/my-rag-app/config \
55	  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
56	  -d '{
57	    "config": {
58	      "provides": [{
59	        "capability": "search",
60	        "http_endpoint": "POST /query",
61	        "bus_channel": "search:results",
62	        "description": "Hybrid RAG search"
63	      }]
64	    }
65	  }'
66	```
67	
68	## Step 2 — Declare what each project consumes
69	
70	```json
71	{
72	  "consumes": [
73	    {
74	      "capability": "search",
75	      "required": false,
76	      "description": "Falls back to direct database query if no search provider"
77	    }
78	  ]
79	}
80	```
81	
82	`required: true` means the project will warn at startup if no provider is registered. `required: false` means the project degrades gracefully.
83	
84	## Step 3 — Discover providers
85	
86	```bash
87	# All capabilities across all projects
88	make list-capabilities
89	
90	# Projects providing a specific capability
91	make list-capabilities CAP=search
92	
93	# Direct API
94	curl http://localhost:3200/capabilities/search
95	```
96	
97	Response:
98	```json
99	{
100	  "capability": "search",
101	  "providers": [
102	    {
103	      "project": "my-rag-app",
104	      "type": "rag",
105	      "capability": "search",
106	      "http_endpoint": "POST /query",
107	      "bus_channel": "search:results",
108	      "description": "Hybrid RAG search"
109	    }
110	  ]
111	}
112	```
113	
114	## Step 4a — Sync routing (HTTP)
115	
116	A consuming project calls a provider's HTTP endpoint directly, using the registry to resolve the URL at startup:
117	
118	```typescript
119	// src/lib/capability-client.ts
120	const API_BASE = process.env.WORKBENCH_API ?? "http://mcp-server:3100";
121	
122	interface Provider {
123	  project: string;
124	  http_endpoint: string;
125	}
126	
127	export async function resolveCapability(name: string): Promise<Provider | null> {
128	  const res = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/capabilities/${name}`);
129	  if (!res.ok) return null;
130	  const data = await res.json();
131	  return data.providers?.[0] ?? null;
132	}
133	
134	export async function callCapability(
135	  capability: string,
136	  body: unknown,
137	  projectHeader?: string
138	): Promise<Response> {
139	  const provider = await resolveCapability(capability);
140	  if (!provider) throw new Error(`No provider for capability '${capability}'`);
141	
142	  const [method, path] = provider.http_endpoint.split(" ");
143	  return fetch(`${API_BASE}${path}`, {
144	    method,
145	    headers: {
146	      "Content-Type": "application/json",
147	      ...(projectHeader ? { "X-Project": projectHeader } : {}),
148	    },
149	    body: JSON.stringify(body),
150	  });
151	}
152	```
153	
154	Usage in a fullstack app's API route:
155	```typescript
156	// Instead of hardcoding the RAG endpoint:
157	const res = await callCapability("search", { question: userQuery }, "my-rag-app");
158	const result = await res.json();
159	```
160	
161	## Step 4b — Async routing (Message Bus)
162	
163	A producing project publishes to the bus; a consuming project subscribes:
164	
165	```typescript
166	// Producer (e.g. data-pipeline project) — publishes after ingesting a document
167	import { busPublish } from "./mcp-client.js";
168	
169	await busPublish("ingest:complete", {
170	  project: "my-rag-app",
171	  filepath: "/workspace/documents/report.pdf",
172	  metadata: { source: "pipeline", date: new Date().toISOString() },
173	});
174	
175	// Consumer (e.g. RAG project) — polls for new ingest jobs
176	import { busRead } from "./mcp-client.js";
177	
178	const messages = await busRead("ingest:complete", { count: 10 });
179	for (const msg of messages) {
180	  await ingestDocument(db, msg.payload.filepath);
181	}
182	```
183	
184	Bus channels follow the convention from the capability declaration (`bus_channel` field). If both sides declare the same channel name, they're already wired — no further configuration needed.
185	
186	## Step 5 — Add a capability to an existing project
187	
188	Use `make add-capability` to merge a new project type's config onto an existing project without touching any files:
189	
190	```bash
191	# Add RAG tools and skills to an existing fullstack project
192	make add-capability NAME=my-fullstack-app CAPABILITY=rag
193	
194	# Add PWA capabilities to an existing fullstack project
195	make add-capability NAME=my-fullstack-app CAPABILITY=pwa
196	
197	# Add mobile scaffold capabilities
198	make add-capability NAME=my-backend CAPABILITY=mobile
199	```
200	
201	This is non-destructive: existing MCP tools, skills, and config are never removed. Only new items from the capability type are added.
202	
203	## Patterns
204	
205	### Pattern A — Peer projects (recommended for production)
206	
207	```
208	┌─────────────────┐    GET /capabilities/search    ┌────────────────┐
209	│  fullstack-app  │ ──────────────────────────────► │   workbench    │
210	│                 │ ◄─────────── providers ──────── │   registry     │
211	│                 │                                  └────────────────┘
212	│                 │    POST /query                   ┌────────────────┐
213	│                 │ ──────────────────────────────► │   rag-project  │
214	│                 │ ◄──────────── results ─────────  │                │
215	└─────────────────┘                                  └────────────────┘
216	```
217	
218	Each project has its own database, lifecycle, and deployment. The fullstack app discovers the RAG project via the registry at startup. If the RAG project goes away, the fullstack app degrades gracefully.
219	
220	### Pattern B — Add capability to same project (simpler, tighter coupling)
221	
222	```bash
223	make add-capability NAME=my-app CAPABILITY=rag
224	```
225	
226	Adds RAG MCP tools directly to the project's config. The project uses its own database for both app data and the knowledgebase. Simpler, but the RAG index and app data share the same lifecycle.
227	
228	### Pattern C — Event-driven pipeline
229	
230	```
231	data-pipeline ──► bus channel: ingest:complete ──► rag-project ──► bus channel: search:ready
232	                                                                  ──► fullstack-app (subscribes)
233	```
234	
235	Each project only knows about the bus channel, not about each other. Adding a new consumer (e.g., a notification service) requires no changes to the pipeline or RAG project.
236	
237	## Checklist
238	
239	- [ ] Both projects are registered (`make list-projects`)
240	- [ ] `provides` entries declared in the providing project's config
241	- [ ] `consumes` entries declared in the consuming project's config
242	- [ ] `make list-capabilities CAP=<name>` confirms the provider is visible
243	- [ ] HTTP routing: `callCapability()` resolves the provider before making the first call
244	- [ ] Bus routing: channel name matches the `bus_channel` in the `provides` declaration
245	- [ ] `required: false` set for capabilities that have graceful fallbacks
246	- [ ] If using `make add-capability`: verify new MCP tools appear in the project config
247	
248	## Files involved
249	
250	| File | Action |
251	|------|--------|
252	| `project.json` (in `.workbench/`) | Update: add `provides` and `consumes` arrays |
253	| `src/lib/capability-client.ts` | Create: resolver + HTTP caller for capabilities |
254	| Any route that calls a peer project | Update: replace hardcoded URL with `callCapability()` |
255	
256	## Common mistakes
257	
258	**Hardcoding the provider project name** — `fetch("http://mcp-server:3100/query?project=my-rag-app")` creates a fixed dependency. Use `callCapability("search")` so the provider can change without touching the consumer.
259	
260	**Forgetting the `X-Project` header** — the workbench API is multi-tenant. If you route to a peer project's endpoint without specifying `X-Project`, the request hits whichever project the server defaults to (often the wrong one). Always pass the provider's project name as the header.
261	
262	**Using Pattern B when lifecycle independence matters** — if the knowledgebase needs to be rebuilt, reindexed, or swapped out independently of the app, it should be a separate project (Pattern A). Merging capabilities into one project makes that harder.
263	
264	**Bus channel naming collisions** — if two unrelated projects both publish to `ingest:complete`, consumers get mixed messages. Namespace channels with the project name for high-traffic channels: `my-pipeline:ingest:complete`.
265	
266	**Not declaring `consumes`** — the registry has no way to warn you if a required capability disappears unless it's declared. Always declare `consumes` entries so `make list-capabilities` can surface mismatches.
267
